articles.headline: Andrej Karpathy Open-Sources ‘Autoresearch’: A 630-Line Python Tool Letting AI Agents Run Autonomous ML Experiments on Single GPUs
articles.description: Andrej Karpathy released autoresearch, a minimalist Python tool designed to enable AI agents to autonomously conduct machine learning experiments. The project is a stripped-down version of the nanochat LLM training core, condensed into a single-file repository of approximately ~630 lines of code. It is optimized for execution on a single NVIDIA GPU. The Autonomous Iteration […] The post Andrej Karpathy Open-Sources ‘Autoresearch’: A 630-Line Python Tool Letting AI Agents Run Autonomous ML Experiments on Single GPUs appeared first on MarkTechPost.

articles.headline: Tech oligarchs reshape humanity while billionaires of old seem quaint
articles.description: From Gates to Musk and Altman, today’s ultra-rich steer AI and tech, raising questions about who decides the futureWhen Bill Gates became the first modern IT mogul to reach the apex of wealth and power in 1992, the world was a very different place. Gates joined the top 10 on Forbes magazine’s billionaires list alongside Japanese, German, Canadian, South Korean and Swedish billionaires, including those with family fortunes from Britain and America. A broad mix of industries was on the list: Retail and media, property management and packaging, an investment firm and a couple of industrial conglomerates. Their fortunes almost added up to $100bn – equivalent to about 0.4% of the US’s GDP that year.The oligarchy has changed drastically since then. Bernard Arnault, of French luxury group LVMH, Amancio Ortega, the Spanish clothing mogul, and Warren Buffett, the US investor, were the only old-school billionaires among the top 10 in 2025. The rest largely made their money from high-tech: Elon Musk, Jeff Bezos, Mark Zuckerberg, Larry Ellison, Steve Ballmer and Google’s Sergey Brin and Larry Page. The top 10 amassed over $16trn, which is about 8% of US GDP. Continue reading...

articles.headline: Trump administration drafts AI contract rules requiring companies to license systems for "all lawful use"
articles.description: New US guidelines for AI contracts would force companies to grant the government an irrevocable license for "all lawful use" and ban ideological bias in AI outputs, which is itself an ideological requirement with striking parallels to China's approach. The article Trump administration drafts AI contract rules requiring companies to license systems for "all lawful use" appeared first on The Decoder.

articles.headline: The OpenClaw superfan meetup serves optimism and lobster
articles.description: The woman at the door wore a plush lobster headdress. She sat in the front hallway of a multistory event venue in Manhattan, beside a bundle of wristbands. If she granted you one, the world of ClawCon beckoned behind her - full of vibey pink and purple lighting, lobster claw headbands, multicolored name tags, sponsor […]

software_engineering.why_care: Your work on any system involving user data or surveillance requires careful consideration of privacy implications and potential public scrutiny.

software_engineering.what_changed: Today's ultra-rich, primarily from the tech sector, are steering the future of AI and technology, contrasting with the diverse industries of past billionaires.
software_engineering.why_care: The increasing influence of tech leaders on AI development directly impacts the tools, platforms, and ethical considerations you will encounter in your work.

software_engineering.headline: Trump Administration Drafts AI Contract Rules Requiring 'All Lawful Use' Licenses
software_engineering.what_changed: New US government guidelines for AI contracts would mandate companies grant an irrevocable license for 'all lawful use' of their AI systems and prohibit ideological bias in outputs.
software_engineering.why_care: These proposed contract rules could significantly impact how you develop, license, and deploy AI systems, especially for government-related projects.
software_engineering.what_to_do: Familiarize yourself with the implications of 'all lawful use' clauses and potential bias requirements in AI development and deployment.
